data.title: Impax U.S. Sustainable Economy Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance

  • The US Sustainable Economy portfolio outperformed the Russell 1000 benchmark during the quarter. Excess returns were driven by a combination of sustainability tilts, and stock specific effects, with style factors marginally detracting. Sector and industry, which are an outcome of the portfolio's sustainability profile, were additive. Stock-specific effects were greatest in Health Care, as pharmaceutical companies including Eli Lilly (LLY) saw outsized gains. Financials were another area of positive stock-specific effects with payment processor Visa (V) and asset manager State Street performing well. Security selection was challenged in Consumer Staples with packaged food names Campbell's (CPB) and General Mills (GIS), as well as household products holding Clorox (CLX), detracting. The portfolio's style factor tilts detracted marginally as the portfolio's higher quality profile was not rewarded, as quality factors saw continued headwinds. The portfolio's lower risk profile was marginally additive as the lower exposure to stocks with higher residual volatility contributed positively, while the lower beta profile offset this somewhat. Exposure to value factors was also marginally positive as stocks with lower price-to-earnings ratios (PE) outperformed higher PE stocks in the period, while the portfolio's slight tilt away from momentum detracted.
  • The sustainability tools were a key driver of portfolio performance in the period, with industry tilts from the Sustainability Lens and our focus on companies with attractive Corporate Resilience profiles both benefiting the portfolio.

Market overview

  • US equity markets, as measured by the Russell 1000, ended the quarter modestly higher. The period was marked by bouts of volatility, driven by concerns over elevated valuations and uncertainty surrounding returns and financing for large-scale data center investments. These factors prompted profit-taking in certain mega-cap technology and AI-related companies, although the market overall remained resilient. Market leadership shifted toward Health Care, which rallied following the Trump administration's agreement with major pharmaceutical firms to reduce Medicaid drug prices. Pro-cyclical sectors also outperformed, with Materials buoyed by commodity strength and Financials expected to benefit from looser monetary conditions. Small caps also performed strongly, reaching new highs, as investors seek out attractive valuations.
  • Softer labor market data and signs of consumer weakness in residential housing led the Federal Reserve (Fed) to deliver two additional interest rate cuts during the quarter, despite persistent inflationary pressures. While some economic indicators softened, US Q3 GDP growth surprised to the upside and corporate earnings remained robust. A new Fed Chair in 2026 is likely to maintain a 'dovish' stance, with further rate cuts highly likely through the year.
  • As we enter 2026, our macroeconomic outlook is one of cautious optimism. Falling interest rates and rising government spending should provide support for risk assets. However, narrow drivers of economic growth and unsustainable fiscal trajectories remain areas of concern. Our investment approach continues to be to build a diversified portfolio of businesses with strong long-term secular growth drivers, sound corporate resilience management, and robust sustainability characteristics.

Contributors

  • Eli Lilly (Health Care, Pharmaceuticals) is included due to its strategic positioning with respect to sustainability opportunities, specifically in HealthCare Access and Innovation. Eli Lilly announced a significant agreement with the Trump administration aimed at improving patient access and reducing prices for select medications. A key development from this agreement is the extended coverage of GLP-1 weight loss drugs within Medicare and Medicaid programs. This announcement was well received by investors, as it alleviated ongoing concerns about stringent drug pricing and created a substantial new market opportunity for GLP-1s.
  • Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) (Information Technology, Semiconductors) is owned for its exceptional Corporate Resilience profile and attractive sustainability opportunity set in areas like the Digital Infrastructure build-out. The stock surged on the back of strong demand for AI-optimized chips and data center processors, which significantly outperformed market expectations. The company also benefited from major partnerships with 'hyperscale' cloud computing providers and record sales of graphics processing units (GPUs) for AI workloads, reinforcing investor confidence in its growth trajectory.
  • Merck (MRK) (Health Care, Pharmaceuticals) demonstrates outstanding Corporate Resilience across governance, environmental and social dimensions. The organization is strategically positioned to leverage opportunities in Health Care Access and Innovation. During the quarter, Merck's shares advanced due to robust sales of its oncology and immunology medications, notably Keytruda, which continued to expand its global market share. Positive clinical trial results and an upward adjustment to 2026 earnings guidance further enhanced market confidence.

Detractors

  • Oracle (ORCL) (Information Technology, Systems Software) stands out with a strong sustainability profile, offering notable opportunities in Digital Infrastructure, Education and the development of Enhanced Skills & Innovation. Recently, the stock declined due to worries that the company is accumulating excessive debt to finance its AI data center expansion supported by OpenAI.
  • Zoetis (ZTS) (Health Care, Pharmaceuticals) is held due to its attractive sustainability opportunity profile, as well as its strong governance profile. There was some disappointment in their Q3 earnings update in November. Zoetis has a full pipeline of new products to launch, but there is some time yet until these products can hit the market and accelerate revenue growth (2027 onwards). In the meantime, they face competition from others to some of their established franchises. Key canine arthritis pain product Librela continues to face side-effect perception issues and declined by more than expected in Q3, leading to disappointment in the quarter.
  • Alphabet Class C (GOOG) (Communication Services, Interactive Media & Services), an underweight position, was one of the top performers in the period. The position is held for its exposure to Digital Infrastructure and Education, Enhanced Skills & Innovation opportunities. Share price performance was driven by robust advertising revenue growth, particularly from YouTube and Search, as digital advertising spending rebounded during the holiday season. Cloud revenues saw double-digit growth and margin improvement, while cost optimization initiatives boosted profitability, leading to positive investor sentiment. The portfolio was overweight the Class A shares at the end of the period.

GICS sector attribution#

  • Rather than following a single risk-on trend, sector performance shifted among various themes such as interest rates, AI, defensives and commodity sensitivity. Only two sectors outperformed the index: Health Care, which benefited from its defensive characteristics and attractive valuations that drew inflows during periods of market uncertainty; and Communication Services, which was supported by Google's AI breakthroughs and potential merger and acquisition (M&A) activity involving Warner Brothers Discovery. Financials delivered mixed results, with capital markets outperforming traditional lenders after the December rate cut. Information Technology (IT) experienced some AI-related indigestion in December due to ongoing concerns about elevated capital expenditures, while Industrials gains were concentrated primarily in segments like logistics, aerospace and defense. Consumer Discretionary performance was divided, with areas supporting higher income consumers showing some resilience, while credit-sensitive stocks tended to weaken in line with softer earnings outlooks. Consumer Staples underperformed as investors weighed the impact of higher prices on middle- and lower-income consumers. Materials were driven by metals and mining equities, reflecting sustained strength in industrial commodity prices. Real Estate, which is typically sensitive to interest rates, might have been expected to benefit from easing rate expectations but continued to face headwinds related to long-term financing concerns. Utilities experienced profit-taking after electric utility stocks delivered strong returns over the calendar year.
  • The portfolio's modest sector tilts aided performance this quarter. Overweighting Health Care (notably Pharmaceuticals and Biotechnology) contributed positively, as did underweight positions in Consumer Staples and Consumer Discretionary. Limited sustainability opportunities in Communication Services and an overweight to IT detracted from results.

Sustainability tools

Impax Sustainability Lens

The Lens is used to align the portfolio's industry exposure to the tailwinds associated with the transition to a more sustainable economy by ranking sub-industries based on the exposure to themes, like Education, Enhanced Skills & Innovation, Meeting Basic Needs, and Addressing Climate Change and Pollution. The portfolio's exposure to the Impax Sustainability Lens contributed positively during the period, as the overweight to stocks in higher opportunity sub-industries was additive. Exposure to high opportunity Health Care industries, like Pharmaceuticals and Biotechnology, was additive, as was exposure to the semiconductor value chain and application software.

Corporate Resilience Analysis

Impax assesses a company's resilience to a variety of industry and company-specific factors. The team's research has found that companies that do not manage governance, the environment, or social risks effectively exhibit more volatility than those with more resilience over the long term. The portfolio maintains an overweight position in stocks with higher Corporate Resilience scores and excludes those with lower scores. This approach contributed positively to performance in the final quarter, with companies scoring highly on Corporate Resilience outperforming the broader market, while those with the lowest scores in the Russell 1000 significantly lagged. The pronounced underweight allocations to stocks with poor Corporate Resilience scores, such as Meta and Palantir – which were excluded due to unfavorable profiles – positively contributed to relative performance, particularly as these stocks generated negative results in an otherwise moderately positive market environment. Conversely, active holdings in high-scoring companies further supported portfolio performance.

Fund overview

Impax US Sustainable Economy Fund is a systematic strategy investing in US large cap companies we believe are positioned to benefit from the transition to a more sustainable economy.

data.title: Pacer Data And Digital Revolution ETF: Another Copycat ETF, No Interest
data.description:
The ticker symbol for the Pacer Data and Digital Revolution ETF (TRFK) is, to me, more about "traffic" than "terrific." I say that tongue-in-cheek, but there's a serious point to be made. And it's not just about this ETF. It's about the industry, broadly.

I'm as big a fan of the ETF structure as you will find. But Wall Street has again done what it tends to do with any good idea: it bludgeons it by overdoing it. Too many products is not a problem. Too many products that do not differentiate from each other IS a problem. At least to me.

Because we investors and investment writers have to explain to the masses why owning the same thing in many different forms is not a good idea. It is just not easy for the untrained eye to see. Unless they spend more time than most investors should and will take.

Let's take TRFK, which I'm the first to cover at Seeking Alpha. Is this a "bad" ETF? Not in the fact that it appropriately targets the theme it sets out to. Here's how the fund, issued by one of the biggies in ETF-land, Pacer, describes itself.

That all sounds exciting, right? And maybe it will continue to be. But the issue to me, with this or any ETF, now that we have more than 4,000 of them in the US alone, is this: "Can I get a similar outcome in products that already exist?" The answer in this case, and in so many others I come across, is YES. Capital letters are intentional there.

Tech ETFs are everywhere. And some are more effective than others. But as in the sport of boxing, if you are going to beat the champ and take the title belt, you need to knock him out. You can't win on "points" from the judges.

Pacer, through TRFK, has every right to trot out as many ETFs as they like, no matter how much they may be just like others out there. Including their own.

Think of it this way: the biggest fund firms have done this for decades for 2 reasons: people naturally flock to something new, especially from a name they trust (think Vanguard, iShares, Invesco, etc.). Even if it is not much different than what they have. Back in the day, funds like Janus and Calamos did this well. At least for them. Shareholders simply owned 2 mutual funds that moved roughly in sync but thought they were "diversified."

And fund companies have a vested interest in allowing their managers (indexed or active) to avoid being so big that their security selection has to be limited to the very largest stocks. It's a liquidity issue in that when an ETF wants to move on from a stock, they don't want it to take multiple days since they own so much of the float.

So along comes TRFK, and it is doing what many others that came before it do. It buys companies that meet a broad definition and weights them by market cap. Then rebalances them every 3 months. Yes, they cap the individual positions at 10%, but that is not a big difference, since the biggest S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 names are below that or close to it.

That produces a portfolio that looks like this. My reaction? Haven't I seen this somewhere before? Like EVERYWHERE? Very familiar, popular, big tech names. And top-heavy like the indexes themselves. Just with a tech focus.

Which brings me to this: is TRFK just another way to own the much more tenured, easy-to-follow State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK)? Not all the time, but too often to make me want to run out and buy TRFK.

This chart shows that TRFK has had a nice run over time. But as I like to say about past performance, we can't have it. It is in the past!

This is the chart that convinces me I can just own XLK and call it a day. For the past year, the 2 ETFs have been in lockstep. That tells me that there was a brief period in which TRFK's top holdings blew the roof off the tech sector. But now that's over. Can you get a little "alpha" from TRFK? Sometimes.

But is it part of what I think many investors should focus more time on in a market climate where correlation is so high? No.

This is an expensive time to be trying to profit over a multi-year period from the tech sector. It takes more than a collection of the "usual suspects" which is what TRFK owns.

A trailing P/E of 44x, 13x trailing sales? No thanks for me. If I truly trusted the potential future earnings growth rates, maybe I'd be watching this to buy in a crash situation. But the way markets now act, I'd be better off just clicking on XLK and buying that instead. To be clear, that ETF looks dreadful to me currently as well.

The best thing I can say about this chart of TRFK is that it is flat. That's

That's better than XLK, shown here. It is starting to look "droopy" (not a technical term). But it's a step away from a leak becoming a flood.

A new ETF should be held to a high bar, a higher standard. TRFK does not do enough to merit consideration from me.

data.title: Netflix Is A Dip Worth Buying With Its Warner Bros. Acquisition
data.description:
Netflix (NFLX) has over 325 million subscribers around the globe, with 98%+ retention. This is an entrenched entertainment company with immense operating leverage and long-term growth. Acquiring Warner Bros. (WBD) allows for higher engagement driven by great IP and industry-best production, large opportunities with HBO, and financial synergies across the company.

Management estimates Netflix's serviceable market is 750 million subscribers. The runway is long not only for customer acquisition but also for engagement and other offerings. Netflix launched an advertising business a few years ago; for 2026, they guided for its revenue to double to $3 billion. While SaaS companies are in a major bear market in fear of AI, Netflix content or distribution can't be vibe-coded. A high-retention subscription and advertising business on sale is hard to come by, due to their predictable and fast growth.

Netflix's business model leaves plenty of operating leverage because of slower content spend and overhead costs growth compared to topline. For years the bear case for Netflix was that it is in a constant hamster flywheel of content spending, that it could never scale profitability. Netflix has successfully blown that narrative out of the water. Over the past decade, revenue has grown at a 21% CAGR vs. content spend CAGR of 11.5% (biggest expense):

Moat

Netflix has won the "streaming wars." Disney or HBO didn't kill them. In fact, no competitor comes close to Netflix's scale. Why? They don't mind disrupting themselves. Netflix started off as a DVD-by-mail rental company before evolving to streaming. Over the past few years, they have created huge cultural shows, a low-cost ad plan for consumers (which generates similar revenue as premium tiers), very successful live events, video games, and recently exclusive video podcasts.

Take live sports, for example: Netflix didn't invest in cable like Disney. Instead, they only host high-volume events like football on holidays or celebrity WWE fights. This all combines into a must-have service for over 325 million subscribers. Smaller competitors lack the capital, scale, or execution to meaningfully gain share. Even Disney has fumbled some of its legendary IP, including Star Wars and Marvel, over the past few years.

Warner Bros. Opportunity

This is a transformative acquisition for Netflix and, surprisingly, wasn't even planned a year ago. After bids started, management decided it would be detrimental if a competitor like Paramount owned WBD and that it could be even more accretive in their own hands. For years Netflix has lacked "legacy IP." They have built amazing original shows like Squid Game or Stranger Things, but premium shows like Game of Thrones or blockbuster movies like Dune were noticeably absent from their content library. Netflix will now be able to rival the likes of Disney with a huge amount of IP at scale.

Notably, Netflix is not acquiring the Discovery portion of Warner Bros. (the decaying cable business). However, they will continue to support the theater business. Theater revenue should be supplemental, as they can monetize big projects before exclusively hosting or licensing them onto streaming platforms.

To quantify the potential media power, during the Golden Globes Nomination,

Netflix and Warner Bros dominated the event, accounting for a mighty 35 and 33 nods respectively.

What happens when a streaming service provides better content? Better engagement, which drives higher retention and ad revenue.

For the financials, this deal costs Netflix $80 billion in cash. Paramount has been rejected at least eight times, with 93% of shareholders voting against their bid, essentially removing them from the picture. The acquisition should close over the next year if approved; regulatory processes have already begun. It won't be easy, but management is confident in its ability to get this deal passed, considering Netflix faces heavy competition from Disney and especially YouTube.

In terms of the fundamentals, management expects it to be accretive to EPS after year two (2029), including $3 billion of cost synergies. For 2026, Netflix will stop buybacks to save up cash for the acquisition and will continue to use its growing FCF to maintain a healthy balance sheet, eventually resuming buybacks in a few years. The net debt/FCF will spike to ~5x but will deleverage quickly to below 3x (a healthy ratio) by 2028. Netflix will then own HBO, not one but two streaming giants. The idea is to double down on HBO premium content and, in some form, bundle it in Netflix. This also lowers licensing fees and creates a stronger ecosystem.

Financials

Management isn't opaque when it comes to communication. They clearly state on their investor website:

Our financial goals are to sustain healthy revenue growth, expand our operating margin and grow free cash flow.

Revenue has grown ~21% on average over the past decade, driven by subscriber growth and price increases. This has naturally decelerated over time. However, it recently picked up to 17% in Q4. In the long term, management wants to grow revenue 10%+. As explained earlier, there's still room to add hundreds of millions of subscribers to Netflix, with HBO duplicating the opportunity. Additionally, 55% of revenue is generated outside of North America; those markets are usually lower priced to attract new subscribers. While Netflix continues offering better value and international markets mature, there is ample pricing power left.

Management wants to reinvest its excess cash flow for the benefit of shareholders. Over the past few years, they have spent almost all profits back into reducing the share count, increasing EPS by 1-2% per year. This will resume after the debt returns to normal levels or the acquisition fails to pass.

Netflix is one of the least dilutive companies in the market, with roughly 3% of its FCF consisting of SBC (and decreasing further over time). For example, Paramount (PSKY) SBC dilutes 78% of FCF, while a mature company like Apple (AAPL) is around 10%. Low dilution helps buybacks become more effective.

Because of Netflix's organic growth, operating leverage, and buyback program, they have aggressively grown EPS by 56% annually over the past decade. While this has decelerated over time, I believe they can continue to grow it 20%+ over the next 5 years.

Valuation

Netflix is trading at a noticeable discount towards its growth and long-term prospects. Management's internal "goals" were leaked last year; during the Q4 call, they said those were through organic measures (not including WBD). These include:

  • $80b revenue, low double-digit growth
  • $30b operating income, triple in five years
  • 415m subscribers, driven by India/Brazil expansion and ad tier

All of which accumulates to their goal of reaching a $1T market cap in 2030, currently ~$325b at $76.

If you believe in management's future execution, then NFLX could return a 25% CAGR if it reaches a $1T market cap in 2030. The nuance here is that these were "goals," not guidance. Additionally, WBD fundamentally changes the company. The acquisition should accelerate earnings power and growth over the long term through cost synergies, HBO expansion, and higher engagement.

My "Fair Value" (FV) assumes 15% CAGR, or double every five years. If a stock is below FV, then it's undervalued, or vice versa. These two base case models, depending on the acquisition outcome, assume a current share price of $76:

WBD Acquisition Fallout

While the path to the acquisition is clear and Paramount is basically out of the picture, regulators still have the power to block this deal from passing. Netflix would continue its organic growth path, only paying a one-time fee of ~$6b. If they achieve their 2030 goals listed above, they can achieve 18% annual net income growth and 2% buyback yield. For a subscription and increasingly advertising company, which is growing substantially above the market with ample operating leverage, a 30x multiple is reasonable. This implies Netflix FV is around $98, ~20% higher than today's price of $76. The current price also provides a margin of safety if management falls short of expectations.

($2.63 EPS instead of $2.53 to adjust for one-time Brazilian tax hit)

Metric Base Case Assumption
Current EPS (TTM) $2.63
Proj. EPS Growth Rate (CAGR) 20%
Terminal EPS Yield 30x
2030 Projected EPS ~$6.55
2030 Stock Price Target ~$195
Implied Return (CAGR) ~20.5%
Intrinsic Value (Today) $97.5

Netflix/WBD Powerhouse

The exact financials are almost impossible to conclude this early on. However, WBD will be accretive to Netflix earnings by 2029 and onwards, according to management. Through the opportunities discussed above, I'm raising EPS growth by 5% and multiple to 33x to reflect the long-term accretive upside, bringing Netflix FV to $131 (or ~60% upside):

Metric Base Case Assumption
Current EPS (TTM) $2.63

Terminal EPS Yield 33x
2030 Projected EPS ~$8
2030 Stock Price Target ~$262
Implied Return (CAGR) ~28%
Intrinsic Value (Today) $131

Risks

Netflix may not successfully maintain Warner Bros. IP and production, causing the acquisition to be value-destructive towards the company's fundamentals. This isn't a simple acquisition. Talent and relationships retention is a major risk. If top-tier directors and industry partners have trouble with these new dynamics, then production quality could decrease.

There is the "king of TV": YouTube. I don't believe YouTube is the killer of Netflix. There is a place for both in modern media. However, if engagement continues to transfer over to YouTube, engagement can suffer as a result, particularly affecting the rising advertising business.

Lastly, if the WBD acquisition simply gets blocked, that can cause a messy stock performance as the market re-rates NFLX, for better or worse. The long-term potential of Netflix decreases without WBD by having less optionality and assets, despite the company having a great thesis before the announcement.

Conclusion

I believe Netflix is a strong buy at its current price of $76. I have a 1-year price target of $100, ~30% higher. Catalysts for a re-rating could be the WBD acquisition being approved, as well as strong execution into 2026. Netflix has won the 'streaming wars,' but the path ahead is far more exciting. The opportunity with WBD is immense, and there is a clear runway of growth ahead, which is why I believe Netflix has a large terminal value and is one of my top picks.

data.title: Bitfarms: A Bitcoin Mining Company In Transition
data.description:
Bitfarms Ltd. (BITF) stands as one of the prominent players in the Bitcoin mining industry, a sector defined by high-stakes energy consumption, technological innovation, and direct correlation to cryptocurrency prices. As of the close of trading on February 13, 2026, BITF shares traded at $2.14, for a market cap of approximately $1.29 billion. The valuation reflected a Bitcoin price of a little under $69,000. Mining stocks like BITF remain under pressure from post-halving economics, rising energy costs, and competitive battles over hash rate.

The Toronto-based company was founded in 2017 and has since transitioned from a Quebec-based hydro-powered miner to a global operator with facilities stretching across North and South America. The company’s focus has been on low-cost, renewable energy sources — primarily hydroelectric power, which has positioned it as a sustainability leader in the industry. Even so, BITF’s journey has not been without ups and downs: from pandemic-era highs above $80 in 2021 (pre-split) to sub-$1 lows in 2022’s crypto winter. Investors considering taking a position in BITF today must weigh its expansion ambitions against macroeconomic headwinds like interest rates and regulatory scrutiny.

The Clarity Act will help with the latter, while interest rates are expected to come down in 2026, which has recently given a boost to the price of Bitcoin.

In this article, we'll take a look at Bitfarms’ history, operations, financial health, stock performance, and future prospects.

Company history and evolution

Bitfarms went public in 2017 on the TSX Venture Exchange under the ticker symbol of BITF. It was originally backed by a number of investors, including Digital Asset Capital Management. Initially, it used inexpensive hydropower in Quebec, Canada, to run its ASIC miners.

By 2019, it was listed on both the Toronto Stock Exchange and NASDAQ, providing the firm with the needed capital to rapidly expand operations.

The 2021 bull market allowed the company to further accelerate growth, resulting in at-the-market capital raised from convertible notes and offerings. That brought about an increase in hash rate from under 1 EH/s to over 6 EH/s by the end of the year.

All of that worked well under optimal market conditions, but in 2022, its business model was challenged by the Bitcoin crash, where the flagship crypto plummeted from roughly $69,000 to about $16,000. That forced the company to cut costs and sell some of its miners, bringing about a decline in revenues.

Among some of its major moves was when it acquired the 30 MW facility in the state of Washington, while recently diversifying itself via high-performance computing and AI workloads at the site.

Growth initiatives

From mid-2022 to early 2023, Bitcoin traded in a range of a little over $16,000 to approximately $23,000, when it finally broke out in the latter part of March 2023 and started soaring in price in October 2023. It didn't stop its upward move until it reached its all-time high two years later.

BITF wasn't able to match Bitcoin's growth trajectory during that time, although it did make a nice upward move in September 2025 before plunging in unison with the Bitcoin correction. If its share price remains subdued, it will continue to be under pressure until the price of Bitcoin begins a sustainable recovery.

While Bitcoin started to show strength again in 2023, BITF expanded into Paraguay, where it was able to operate with cheaper energy, costing about $0.03/kWh.

In 2024, the company competed at 12 EH/s of installed hash rate, with its $150 million capital raise helping to finance that growth. With the 2025 acquisition of Stronghold Digital Mining’s Pennsylvania assets for $55 million, it added some coal-refurbished sites to its holdings. Afterward, CEO Ben Gagnon stated the company had a target of 21 EH/s by the middle of 2026.

More recently, BITF sold off its 70 MW site in Paso Pe, Paraguay, to Sympatheia Power Fund for $30 million. It was to receive $9 million in cash at close, the remaining $21 million over the next ten months. Maybe the challenges of droughts in the region triggered, at least in part, its decision.

Last but not least, the company announced on February 6, 2026, that it had plans to redomicile in the U.S. from Canada, rebranding as Keel Infrastructure because of its ongoing transition from Bitcoin mining to AI infrastructure.

Even though the company reported a weak Q3 in November 2025, with revenue coming in at $69.25 million, missing by $15.41 billion, and EPS of -$0.08, missing by $0.06, this period of the company's evolution makes it difficult to value the company.

Also, gross mining margin dropped from 44% in Q3 2024 to 35% in the reporting period. The point is, more of its business will come from outside of Bitcoin mining, and how the company will be able to compete against some of its largest competitors remains to be seen.

I like the needed refocus of its business, but it has opened up another competitive front it has yet to prove its long-term execution and performance in.

While true, BITF COO Liam Wilson is bullish on the prospects of the company, stating at the 28th Annual Needham Growth Conference, January 13, 2026, that "our megawatts are harder to get in higher demand areas, produce more value for customers, and are worth more per megawatt."

If the company can scale that, it could be a growth engine for the prolonged future.

Conclusion

BITF launched as a vertically integrated, inexpensive miner to scale globally. While it will remain a Bitcoin mining operation for some time, that will undoubtedly decline in importance as revenue and earnings from that unit of the company decline going forward.

As mentioned earlier, growth will increasingly come from demand for data infrastructure driven by AI, as well as demand for high‑performance computing (HPC). The market, in general, likes what the company is doing, but its success is far from being guaranteed.

Not only is competition in the Bitcoin mining sector brutal, with larger competitors like CleanSpark, Core Scientific, Marathon, and RIOT dominating the sector, scaling far beyond BITF. That means it must compete on lower costs when mining BTC-USD, extremely efficient facility management, and expansion in a way that doesn't totally rely upon hash rate in and of itself. In the AI compute segment of the market, the competition is even more fierce, where BITF goes beyond directly competing with larger Bitcoin mining firms to competing with large data-center operators, cloud providers, and GPU startups with significant backing from venture capital.

For the company to succeed, it must compete within the confines of its cost structure. If it can do so with GPU hosting and/or HPC workloads, it could potentially thrive in that specific niche of the market.

To do so, management has to execute close to flawlessly. It could possibly get by with a few smaller hiccups, but any large mistake against the powerhouses it's competing against on the mining, hosting, and GPU sides of the business, and it would likely struggle to recover.

If that were to play out, the best hope for shareholders would be for the company to be acquired.

And speaking of being acquired, I tend to think the steps it is taking are anticipating a potential offer from a larger suitor.

It could definitely thrive in the niche it's gravitating toward, again, assuming it can execute at a high level, but I think there's a ceiling on its upside growth in the future as the company stands, and there isn't much clarity on what it'll do if and when it successfully executes its current business plan.

data.title: Coinbase: Betting On A Correction Is A Coin Toss
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Coming into 2025, Bitcoin and the broader market were declining, and then we saw prices bottoming in the first week of April, followed by a healthy correction. We're seeing the similar trend coming into 2026; however, the broader market seems to be holding up pretty well while crypto prices continue to witness massive drawdowns. Year-to-date, the (SPY) is up 0.5%, while Bitcoin and Coinbase (COIN) lost 21% and 27% of value, respectively. Coinbase's price trades closely to Bitcoin's price but with a higher volatility, as it is perceived to be a more risk-on asset compared to Bitcoin given the management and the operational risk embedded in the equity. The reason for the strong correlation between two assets has to do with crypto trading volume. Generally, when the Bitcoin price appreciates, we witness more trading activity among exchanges, which translates to more volume and more revenue. The opposite occurs when Bitcoin drops.

YoY, COIN lost 48.68% of its value, while Bitcoin lost 32.83%, and the S&P returned 14.23%. For the knife-catchers, the way to play the trade would be to buy Coinbase, as it's the stock that could witness steeper upside in the case of an industry correction. I wouldn't recommend such a trade for conservative investors like myself, as I see structural headwinds that limit the upside potential. If you want to play the "This could be the bottom" game, I think Bitcoin is a safer asset; hence, I'm rating COIN a hold.

Bear Case

The main bear case for Coinbase is that the firm still sources the majority of its revenue from retail trading volume. This is the business segment that has been in a cyclical decline since the peak of 2021. Institutional trading has also been in a decline but is slowly returning to the same level as in 2021. From Dec. 2021, retail trading volume decreased 55%, from 535 billion to 239 billion, and institutional trading volume only decreased 14%, from 1.14 trillion to 982 billion. The YoY growth rate in 2021 was over 600% for retail trading and over 700% for institutional trading; the second peak was witnessed in December of 2024, at 198.67% for retail trading and 145.55% for institutional trading. As of the last quarter, the retail growth rate is at 6.70%, and the institutional growth rate is at 1.76%, with signs of dipping into the negative territory as crypto activity continues to slow.

There are two reasons why Coinbase witnesses these volume declines, especially from the retail side. The first reason is that Coinbase still charges retail a higher take rate compared to its institutional client, 1.50% vs. 0.05% as of the last quarter. This opens a room for competitors like Robinhood (HOOD) and Hyperliquid to take some of its retail base by offering lower transaction fees.

According to Artemis Analytics, Coinbase, in line with Robinhood, charges a take rate well above 1%, while Hyperliquid charges below 0.1%. As a result, Hyperliquid has recently surpassed Coinbase in trading volume, proving that retail is willing to switch service providers to reduce costs, especially now that most crypto portfolios are in the red. I see this headwind not as cyclical but structural; what's only offsetting the decline is the slow adoption of DEX in the US due to lack of regulatory clearance.

The second reason is that crypto is losing retail market share to other trading/gambling avenues like sports betting, options, and prediction markets. Crypto has few use cases outside of trading, namely, cross-border payments and remittances, but trading still accounts for the majority of the use cases. The problem with trading as a use case is that the demand side is very fickle; once opportunities to make easy money disappear, so do the participants. To address this problem, Coinbase has decided to shift its mission from being a crypto gateway to being an "everything exchange" that will facilitate all kinds of trading on one platform. These are mostly matured markets with fierce competition, so for Coinbase to execute a good go-to-market strategy, it will have to get into an acquisition spree of smaller players that have already achieved product-market fit, e.g., the acquisition of Deribit, introducing capital and managerial risks into the equity.

Bull Case

The bull case for Coinbase is that the firm is relying less and less on trading volumes as the source of revenue and is growing its services segment, which brings high-margin recurring revenue. The impact of the services revenue on the top line is already apparent. Looking at the trading volume and the transaction revenue charts, we can see that the rebound from the 2023 bottom was subtle; however, Coinbase revenue rebounded significantly, and the main driver of the rebound was subscription and services revenue, which has been steadily increasing over the years. In December 2021, Coinbase revenue peaked at $7.84 billion on a TTM basis, with $6.84 billion coming from transaction revenue and $517.49 million coming from services revenue (7% of revenue share). As of the fourth quarter of 2025, TTM revenue is at $6.88 billion, with $4.06 billion coming from transaction revenue and $2.83 billion coming from services revenue (41% of revenue).

One headwind that is likely to face the services segment is that, as of the last earnings result, stablecoin revenue accounted for 48% of total subscription and services revenue, making this segment highly sensitive to interest rate changes. Given that there's a higher expectation of the Fed cutting rates in the coming years, this could impact Coinbase's topline growth.

Financial Analysis

Q4 2025 Income Statement

Looking at the last earnings result, Coinbase's revenue declined 22% YoY to $1.781 million, missing estimates by $46.1 million. This double-digit decline was caused by retail revenue decreasing 46% to $733.88 million and block rewards revenue decreasing 29% to $151.63 million. Offset by institutional revenue growing 31% to $184.95 million and stablecoin revenue growing 61% to $364.13 million. Aggregate transactional revenue declined 37% to $982.65 million, and services revenue increased 13% to $727.39 million.

Transactional expenses decreased 31% to $218.63 million, resulting in gross profits declining 20% to $1.56 billion and landing gross margins at 87.7%. Operating expenses increased 40% to $1.29 billion, resulting in operating income decreasing 74% to $273.75 million and landing EBIT margins at 15%, below the sector median of 24%. Finance expenses increased 103% to $1.16 billion, largely influenced by the price depreciation in crypto assets held on the balance sheet, which resulted in an operational loss of $886.31 million.

Full-Year 2025 Income Statement

Looking at the 2025 full fiscal year, transaction revenue increased 2% to $4.06 billion; the revenue growth was offset by retail revenue decreasing 3% to $3.32 billion. Services revenue increased 23% to $2.83 billion, bolstered by stablecoin revenue increasing 48% to $1.35 billion. Overall, total revenue increased 9% to $7.18 billion.

Operating expenses increased 41% to $4.73 billion, bolstered by the sales and marketing budget increasing 62% to $1.06 billion, resulting in operating income decreasing 38% to $1.44 billion, pretax income decreasing 48% to $1.52 billion, and net income decreasing 51% to $1.26 billion. Bulls would say these margin contractions are cyclical, and once crypto markets correct, margins will expand again. I see this as a structural headwind given the maturation of the crypto space, inviting more competitors offering lower take rates to retail.

Valuation

For the first quarter of 2026, analysts estimate revenue to be at $1.59 billion, implying an 18% revenue decline relative to the prior year quarter and further margin compression to the bottom line. I find these estimates to be fair, as bear markets tend to last more than 3 quarters. Given the structural headwinds I listed, Coinbase still trades expensively relative to its sector median, given the D- rating; however, most of its industry peers are concentrated in the F rating, proving that the premium valuation is not only idiosyncratic to Coinbase but also a broader industry mispricing.

Conclusion

Even though COIN closely tracks the price of Bitcoin, unlike Bitcoin, the headwinds for the firm are more structural and less cyclical, and it seems that the markets have priced this in, given the massive drawdown witnessed. Even if crypto markets recover, I am highly skeptical that this will immediately turn bullish for Coinbase as they continue to lose market share of retail trading amid the risks of rate cuts for that services business. I don't expect further drawdowns, as I think the stock has received enough market punishment, but I also don't see a massive correction for 2026; hence, a neutral position on this name is warranted.

data.title: Eurozone Industrial Upturn To Continue Despite December Dip
data.description:
By Peter Vanden Houte, Chief Economist, Belgium, Luxembourg, Eurozone

Eurozone industrial production fell in December, but the cyclical upturn is likely to continue on the back of stronger domestic demand

Structural headwinds won't stop the cyclical recovery

Eurozone industrial production fell by 1.4% month-on-month in December but still grew by 1.2% on a year-on-year basis. The month-on-month decline was largely expected, as the recently published German and French figures were weak for December.

It is easy to be downbeat about the prospects for eurozone industrial growth, as structural headwinds remain important. Natural gas prices are still more than three times as high in Europe as in the US, and cheap Chinese exports to Europe continue to grow at a strong pace. At the same time, European exporters still face higher import tariffs in the US. In the January business sentiment survey from the European Commission, the assessment of export orders remained downbeat, but overall orders improved. This suggests that domestic demand within the eurozone is starting to pick up, with German stimulus plans likely to be an important driving force. Indeed, German industrial orders climbed nearly 20% in the last four months of 2025.

There are also signs that the inventory correction is largely behind us, with the assessment of stocks now close to the historical average. We therefore expect manufacturing to contribute positively to eurozone growth this year, even though the structural issues are far from resolved.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: S&P Global: An Undervalued Dividend King For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) is a US-based leading provider of credit ratings, benchmarks, analytics, and workflow solutions to various markets worldwide. Founded in 1860, S&P Global is now a $133 billion (by market cap) financial markets behemoth that employs more than 40,000 people. Approximately 60% of the company’s revenue is derived from the US. The company reports results across five segments: Market Intelligence, 32% of FY 2024 revenue; Ratings, 30%; Commodity Insights, 15%; Mobility (which is being spun out), 11%; and Indices, 11%.

I view S&P Global as three wonderful businesses all in one: S&P Global has a scaled-up intelligence business with high-value proprietary data; it controls the largest ratings agency in the world; and it owns an irreplaceable indices business with various lionized S&P products under its umbrella (such as the venerable S&P 500, the global gold standard). Each business on its own would be very powerful and highly appealing for almost any long-term investor, but all three being combined into one package is what makes S&P Global one of the most unique and compelling long-term investment opportunities out there.

In data-driven markets where information is prized for insight, S&P Global has a treasure trove of proprietary data at unparalleled scale. Since companies issuing public debt are practically required to have a rating, and since there are only two global players providing these ratings at a trusted scale, S&P Global operates within a comfortable duopoly (with S&P Global estimated as commanding a market share of nearly 50%, and its only other major competitor commanding an estimated 30%).

And its indices business has numerous recurring revenue drivers all on its own, including subscription fees, asset-linked fees, and transaction royalties – and all of these are firmly embedded within global capital markets in such a way that it is almost impossible to displace. These are three formidable businesses on a standalone basis, but S&P Global has combined them into a complementary flywheel.

In a world where cycles are shorter and disruption is happening faster, the kind of durability that S&P Global possesses by combining three almost-unassailable businesses under one roof means it’s hard to imagine a world decades into the future in which S&P Global is not larger, more profitable, and more successful than it is today. To the contrary, I believe it’ll continue to pull its many levers to generate substantial revenue, profit, and dividend growth over the decades ahead.

Dividend Growth, Growth Rate, Payout Ratio and Yield

To that point, S&P Global has already increased its dividend for 53 consecutive years. Incredible. That’s one of the longest dividend growth streaks in existence. It easily qualifies S&P Global for its status as a vaunted Dividend Aristocrat and Dividend King. If anything, this is the Dividend Aristocrat (it’s this company’s index, after all). Its 10-year dividend growth rate of 11.3% is very solid, especially considering how that had come after 40 prior years of dividend growth already.

However, more recent dividend raises over the last few years have been much more modest as a result of acquisitions designed to strengthen the company’s grip over global markets (which was already strong before). A payout ratio of 28.2% shows us an extremely healthy dividend poised to grow at a high rate over the coming years.

This foundation is further reinforced by the business’s high rate of growth. Pairing such a low payout ratio with a business growing at a very rapid rate are dual tailwinds positioning shareholders for huge dividend growth over the years ahead. If there’s a drawback here, it’s probably the stock’s yield of 1%.

That’s not exactly a mouthwatering yield, but it is 20 basis points higher than its own five-year average. There’s more immediate yield than usual to be had here, along with a nice setup for dividend growth now that S&P Global is on the other side of some heavy acquisitive activity. This is looking like an opportune moment to invest in a king among kings.

Revenue and Earnings Growth

As opportune as it may be, though, we’re largely relying on past data. However, investors must constantly be considering the future, as the capital of today ultimately gets risked for the rewards of tomorrow. As such, I’ll now build out a forward-looking growth trajectory for the business, which will be taken into account during the valuation process.

I’ll first show you what the business has done over the last decade in terms of its top-line and bottom-line growth. And I’ll then reveal a professional prognostication for near-term profit growth. Lining up the proven past with a future forecast in this manner should give us the ability to roughly estimate where the business may be going from here.

S&P Global moved its revenue from $5.7 billion in FY 2016 to $15.3 billion in FY 2025. That’s a compound annual growth rate of 11.6%. Excellent top-line growth, although a meaningful jump in revenue occurred after S&P Global acquired IHS Markit in 2020 for $44 billion in an effort to significantly expand its data, increase recurring data, and open up cross-selling opportunities.

Meanwhile, earnings per share grew from $7.94 to $17.83 (adjusted) over this period, which is a CAGR of 9.4%. The IHS Markit acquisition muddies the waters, making a straight-line comparison somewhat inaccurate. If we zoom in and look at the most recent quarter (Q4 FY 2025), which is more current and relevant, S&P Global powered ahead with 14% YOY adjusted EPS growth. That’s a bit more like it.

Looking ahead, CFRA is projecting a 12% CAGR for S&P Global’s EPS over the next three years. That includes S&P Global’s upcoming spin-off of its Mobility unit (one of its smallest), which focuses on automotive intelligence. CFRA highlights S&P Global’s dominant position in credit ratings, its standing as the benchmark in indices, and its proprietary data. S&P Global has unrivaled brand power in not one but two industries (ratings and indices).

This is the index company. This is the ratings agency. Its data is the data. In my view, its three main businesses are nearly unassailable. Of course, there is the elephant in the room: AI. While CFRA concedes that questions remain regarding just how competitive and valuable proprietary data will be in an age of AI, S&P Global is busy embedding AI in products and partnering with LLMs in order to fortify the business.

How much of a friend or foe AI will be remains to be determined, but I can’t imagine we’re going to live in a world in which all IP is just scraped freely by third-party apps. And there’s much here that AI simply cannot do. Nobody is going to just “vibe code” the S&P 500. If we assume that CFRA is in the ballpark with its 12% number, that positions S&P Global for mid-teens dividend growth over the coming years by virtue of the payout ratio being so low.

Again, the 1% yield isn’t much, but those who appreciate high-quality compounders should like what they see. S&P Global’s stock has a 10-year CAGR (including reinvested dividends) of 17.7% – even after the recent drawdown – so it’s clearly been (and appears to remain) a high-caliber long-term investment for those willing to accept a low starting yield.

Financial Position

Moving over to the balance sheet, S&P Global has a great financial position. The long-term debt/equity ratio is 0.3, while the interest coverage ratio is nearly 20. Its credit ratings (by the other agencies) are well into investment-grade territory: A3, Moody’s; A-, Fitch. While the balance sheet isn’t quite the indomitable fortress it was a decade ago, it’s still a pillar of strength.

Profitability is robust. Return on equity has averaged 226.2% over the last five years, while net margin has averaged 29%. As impressive as these metrics are, they were even better before the IHS Markit acquisition. ROIC was reliably over 40% before that acquisition, for example, which is otherworldly. S&P Global had some room for a mistake here, so time will tell how much post-AI erosion occurs.

The good news is that the indices and ratings businesses are so good that any possible shareholder value destruction can be absorbed over time. Overall, from what I see, S&P Global is one of the best companies on the planet. And with economies of scale, network effects, barriers to entry, “sticky” products already firmly embedded into global markets, IP, and R&D, the company does benefit from durable competitive advantages.

Of course, there are risks to consider. Litigation, regulation, and competition are omnipresent risks in every industry. S&P Global is somewhat insulated from competition, as it operates (or even dominates) within oligopolistic markets. In addition, because of the regulatory hurdles involved in the global financial markets, along with the way in which S&P Global is so entrenched already, regulation is, arguably, as much of a shield as it is a risk for S&P Global.

The company’s massive trove of data could become less useful and valuable as AI-powered research becomes ubiquitous, although AI is trained on data and proprietary data/IP can’t simply be stolen. AI is currently considered to be a general, broader risk to this entire industry, but it’s unclear exactly what AI can do to dislodge embedded global indices and/or ratings agencies.

IHS Markit was S&P Global’s largest acquisition ever, and there are unanswered questions regarding just how synergistic and beneficial the deal was, along with whether or not the price paid was appropriate. The number of public companies has been decreasing for years, reducing opportunities for listings and ratings, but deal flow appears to be picking up after a lull (which increases the opportunity set for ratings).

Being a global enterprise, S&P Global does face geopolitics and currency exchange rates; however, its lack of physical infrastructure and the embedded nature of its financial products protect the firm from most geopolitical storms. Other than the IHS Markit questions, the risk profile of S&P Global does not strike me as high.

But with the stock in a severe drawdown – dropping by nearly 25% YTD alone – there seems to be a lot of consternation and worry now being priced into shares…

Valuation

The stock now has a P/E ratio of 21.9, based on adjusted EPS. That is nearly half of its own five-year average P/E ratio of 38.5. Its cash flow multiple of 19.6, which is not demanding at all even in isolation, is far lower than its own five-year average of 30.7. And the yield, as noted earlier, is higher than its own recent historical average.

So the stock looks cheap when looking at basic valuation metrics. But how cheap might it be? What would a rational estimate of intrinsic value look like?

I valued shares using a dividend discount model analysis. I factored in a 10% discount rate, a 10-year dividend growth rate of 15%, and a long-term dividend growth rate of 8%. The model includes a near-term acceleration in dividend growth relative to the proven 10-year dividend growth rate, but that 10-year rate has been negatively impacted by unusually small dividend raises over the last several years.

If we go back to before the IHS Markit acquisition, S&P Global was handing out 20% dividend raises like candy. I’m basically assuming a return to the status quo as it pertains to long-run dividend growth out of S&P Global. With the major acquisition already integrated, a low double-digit EPS growth rate being projected over the next few years, and the payout ratio being as low as it is, S&P Global has plenty of headroom to deliver a mid-teens type of dividend growth rate from here.

Of course, I wouldn’t expect that kind of growth to persist forever, and the model does allow for a natural slowdown into a high-single digit level, which I think is appropriate for the quality and growth that S&P Global exudes. The DDM analysis gives me a fair value of $376.75. The reason I use a dividend discount model analysis is because a business is ultimately equal to the sum of all the future cash flow it can provide.

The DDM analysis is a tailored version of the discounted cash flow model analysis, as it simply substitutes dividends and dividend growth for cash flow and growth. It then discounts those future dividends back to the present day to account for the time value of money, since a dollar tomorrow is not worth the same amount as a dollar today. I find it to be a fairly accurate way to value dividend growth stocks. While this has consistently been a very expensive stock, I think it’s now at least in the realm of fairness after an epic drawdown.

But we’ll now compare that valuation with where two professional stock analysis firms have come out at. This adds balance, depth, and perspective to our conclusion.

Morningstar, a leading and well-respected stock analysis firm, rates stocks on a 5-star system. 1 star would mean a stock is substantially overvalued; 5 stars would mean a stock is substantially undervalued. 3 stars would indicate roughly fair value. Morningstar rates SPGI as a 5-star stock, with a fair value estimate of $570.00.

CFRA is another professional analysis firm, and I like to compare my valuation opinion to theirs to see if I’m out of line. They similarly rate stocks on a 1-5 star scale, with 1 star meaning a stock is a strong sell and 5 stars meaning a stock is a strong buy. 3 stars is a hold. CFRA rates SPGI as a 3-star “HOLD” with a 12-month target price of $465.00.

I came out low, surprisingly. Averaging the three numbers out gives us a final valuation of $470.58, which would indicate the stock is possibly 20% undervalued.

Bottom line: S&P Global Inc. is a world-class company made up of three formidable businesses which have been complementarily combined into a fortress that’s nearly unassailable. The fundamentals are among the best I’ve seen. It’s the Dividend Aristocrat. With a market-like yield, double-digit dividend growth, a very low payout ratio, more than 50 consecutive years of dividend increases, and the potential that shares are 20% undervalued, long-term dividend growth investors would be wise to seriously consider this Dividend King now.

Note from D&I: How safe is SPGI’s dividend? We ran the stock through Simply Safe Dividends, and as we go to press, its Dividend Safety Score is 99. Dividend Safety Scores range from 0 to 100. A score of 50 is average, 75 or higher is excellent, and 25 or lower is weak. With this in mind, SPGI’s dividend appears Very Safe with a very unlikely risk of being cut.

Disclosure: I’m long SPGI.

Original Post
